title: Cat Swollen Hock Joint Warm to Touch: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat's hock swollen and warm? Learn about potential causes, urgency levels, and why a photo is vital for veterinary triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: A swollen, warm hock joint in cats indicates inflammation from injury or infection and typically requires a veterinary visit within 24 to 48 hours unless the cat is unable to bear weight or stops eating.

What causes my cat to have a swollen and warm hock joint?

The hock joint is the complex structure on your cat's back leg that functions similarly to a human ankle. When this area becomes swollen and feels warm to the touch, it is a clinical sign of inflammation. This is commonly caused by localized trauma, a bacterial infection such as an abscess from a bite wound, or a flare-up of feline arthritis. The warmth indicates that the body is sending extra blood flow to the area to fight infection or repair tissue damage.

Is a swollen hock joint in a cat considered a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level for a swollen, warm hock is currently classified as Medium.
  • Your cat should be seen by a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours, though it may not require immediate emergency care in the next hour.
  • Escalate the visit to an urgent care or emergency facility if your cat is hiding, refusing food, or is completely unable to put weight on the leg.
  • Immediate care is necessary to address potential systemic infection or underlying fractures.

How does providing a photo help a veterinarian triage my cat's swollen leg?

  • Allows a veterinary professional to compare the swollen hock to the healthy one for better context.
  • Helps a triage expert identify the exact location of the swelling and look for drainage or puncture wounds.
  • Assists in determining the severity of the inflammation through visual evidence.
  • Crucial in deciding whether your pet can wait for a scheduled appointment or needs immediate intervention.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Polyarthritis in cats, involving inflammation of multiple joints, can manifest as swollen joints, and may be accompanied by fever, lethargy, and inappetence. It is classified as infectious (septic arthritis) or noninfectious (erosive or nonerosive, immune-mediated). Radiography can aid diagnosis by revealing joint effusion and possible erosive bone destruction.

Chapter: Musculoskeletal

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1198)

faqs.question: Can I give my cat aspirin for the swelling?
faqs.answer: No. Never give your cat human pain medications like aspirin, ibuprofen, or Tylenol, as these are highly toxic to cats and can be fatal.

faqs.question: What are the most common causes of hock swelling?
faqs.answer: The most common causes include bite wound abscesses, soft tissue injuries, ligament sprains, or inflammatory joint diseases like arthritis.

faqs.question: Should I try to wrap the leg?
faqs.answer: Avoid wrapping or bandaging the leg yourself, as an improper wrap can restrict blood flow or trap bacteria in an infection, making the condition worse.
